text: This article analyses the cultures of militant leftism developed in the public space of France, Italy and West Germany during the ‘red decade’ (1968–1979). While sharing identical strategic objectives and vocabulary concerning counter-violence and self-protection, the militant left in each country performed activism in a manner that was heavily conditioned by national dynamics. In the light of a transnational historical perspective and a comparative approach, this article attempts to elucidate how national specificities – such as past anti-fascist resistance, local levels of social and industrial struggle, and the degree of neo-fascist provocation – were linked to law-enforcement practices. It shows how the latter directly affected the degree of violence exercised by protesters. For example, the comparative evidence reveals that the frequent use of firearms by the Italian police led to a form of escalation in which the left not only armed itself for self-protection but gradually endorsed the use of armed actions in demonstrations, thus moving from the level of the mass protest to that of armed terrorism. Including the French case in the comparative analysis also complicates the terrorist studies’ common teleological approach that posits that activist movements inevitably turn into armed revolutionary militias because of the inescapable nature of the militant ideology and because terrorism is the only efficient mode of armed struggle. Rather than ideology, state violence explains why left-wing activism in France evolved into radicalism but not into terrorism. The French exception illustrates that the activation of the terrorist option was a fragile and historically contingent process, in which the general principle of the counter-violent culture of the left turned into a practice of armed struggle only when ordinary police actions implied and permitted the use of lethal force on the part of protesters.
